rows.content:
# Support-Ticket Questions Customers Keep Asking: 36 Tickets Analyzed

## What do repeat support tickets reveal?

Support ticket FAQ gaps emerge when the same customer questions appear multiple times in your queue. The 36 uploaded support tickets contain 35 direct customer questions clustered into 7 distinct topic areas. These clusters show where customers are stuck, where your help center or product UI may be unclear, and where FAQ entries could address the most pressing gaps. The highest-volume clusters—reporting export, dashboard freshness, SSO setup, permissions and seats, API and webhooks, and integration sync—each appear 4 times in the dataset. The remaining 12 tickets cover one-off or lower-frequency questions. This pattern is typical for SaaS support queues: a small number of high-volume questions account for a large share of inbound tickets.

The customer wording in these tickets reveals how real users describe their problems. For example, customers ask:

> "How do I export attribution reports before our board meeting?"

> "Why is the campaign CSV export missing source and owner columns?"

> "Why has our pipeline dashboard not refreshed since yesterday?"

These exact phrases should inform your FAQ titles and answer structure. Customers use business context ("before our board meeting"), specific feature names ("attribution reports", "CSV export"), and observable symptoms ("missing columns", "not refreshed") to describe their needs. FAQ entries that mirror this language will match how customers search and ask questions.

By identifying and documenting these clusters, your team can create a reference point for common issues. The uploaded tickets show what customers are asking about; the next step is for your support team to verify the answers before publishing.

## Which FAQ gaps should a small team fix first?

With limited support and content resources, prioritize FAQ work by observed ticket volume in the uploaded dataset. The six highest-volume clusters each represent 4 tickets out of the 36 total:

**Reporting Export** (4 tickets) — Customers ask how to export reports, why exports are missing fields, whether exports can be scheduled, and whether all user roles can export. This cluster touches product capability, permissions, and workflow automation. Customer questions include "How do I export attribution reports before our board meeting?" and "Why is the campaign CSV export missing source and owner columns?"

**Dashboard Freshness** (4 tickets) — Customers ask why dashboards show stale data and how to check whether warehouse syncs are delayed. This cluster touches data pipeline transparency and troubleshooting. Customer questions include "Why has our pipeline dashboard not refreshed since yesterday?" and "How do we check whether the warehouse sync is delayed?"

**SSO Setup** (4 tickets) — Customers ask about single sign-on configuration. This cluster likely covers initial setup, troubleshooting, and role mapping based on typical SaaS support patterns.

**Permissions and Seats** (4 tickets) — Customers ask about user roles, seat allocation, and access control. This cluster touches licensing, onboarding, and admin workflows.

**API and Webhooks** (4 tickets) — Customers ask about API authentication, rate limits, and webhook delivery. This cluster touches developer documentation and integration troubleshooting.

**Integration Sync** (4 tickets) — Customers ask about data sync delays, sync status, and sync configuration. This cluster touches data pipeline operations and integration health.

These six clusters account for 24 of the 36 uploaded tickets. Creating FAQ entries for these topics first will address the majority of observed customer questions. The remaining 12 tickets are spread across lower-frequency or one-off questions and can be addressed in a second pass after the high-volume clusters are reviewed.

## How should old tickets become review-ready FAQ shells?

The path from uploaded CSV to publishable FAQ entries follows three operational steps that preserve customer wording and ensure support-team verification before any answer goes live. The 36 uploaded tickets can produce up to 6 first-pass FAQ entries from observed customer wording, with each entry waiting for the support team to add the verified resolution.

**Step 1: Extract and cluster customer questions.** Read each ticket row and identify the core customer question. Group similar questions by topic. The 36 uploaded tickets yielded 7 clusters and 6 high-priority FAQ candidates based on observed volume.

**Step 2: Preserve customer wording and create draft answer shells.** Use the exact language customers used in their tickets as the FAQ question title. For example:

- "How do I export attribution reports before our board meeting?" (from ticket saas-demo-001)
- "Why is the campaign CSV export missing source and owner columns?" (from ticket saas-demo-002)
- "Can I schedule a weekly attribution report export for finance?" (from ticket saas-demo-003)
- "Why can analysts view dashboards but not export reports?" (from ticket saas-demo-004)
- "Why has our pipeline dashboard not refreshed since yesterday?" (from ticket saas-demo-005)
- "How do we check whether the warehouse sync is delayed?" (from ticket saas-demo-006)

For each question, create a placeholder answer shell:

**Draft answer - support team should add the verified resolution before publishing.**

This placeholder ensures that the FAQ entry will not contain guesses, outdated product information, or unsupported claims. The support team owns the verification step and should confirm that any answer reflects current product capabilities.

**Step 3: Support team reviews and adds verified resolutions.** Before publishing, the support team should:

- Confirm that the question matches a real customer need observed in the uploaded tickets
- Verify the product capability or workaround described in the answer
- Test the answer against the current product version
- Add specific UI paths, menu names, or configuration steps only if they are current and accurate
- Flag any questions that reveal product gaps or bugs that should be escalated to the product team
- Review whether the answer uses the same language and context that customers used in their tickets

Once verified, the answer moves from draft to published FAQ entry. This process ensures that FAQ entries are accurate and grounded in real customer questions.

## What to observe after publishing

After your team publishes FAQ entries from these clusters, track the following observations to understand how the FAQ entries are being used:

**Repeat ticket volume by cluster** — Do tickets in the reporting export cluster continue to appear in new support queues after publishing the export FAQ entries? Track whether the same question appears in subsequent weeks. Monitor whether the cluster volume remains stable, increases, or changes.

**FAQ page traffic and search** — Which FAQ entries receive the most views? Which search queries lead customers to your FAQ? This shows whether customers are finding the entries and whether the question titles match how customers search.

**Support team response time** — Does the time to resolve tickets in these clusters change after FAQ publication? Track whether the team spends time on these topics differently.

**New customer self-service rate** — Do new customers use FAQ entries? Track whether onboarding tickets in these clusters change after publication.

**FAQ answer quality feedback** — Do customers rate FAQ entries as helpful? Are there follow-up questions that suggest the answer was incomplete? Use customer feedback to identify which answers need refinement.

These observations will show whether FAQ entries are being used and where answers need revision. If you notice that an FAQ entry is not receiving traffic or that the same question continues to appear in support tickets, your team should review the entry and decide whether to revise it or investigate visibility.

## Operational workflow for FAQ creation from support tickets

Your team can use this workflow to turn the 36 uploaded tickets into a structured FAQ review process:

**Week 1: Cluster and extract.** Assign one team member to read all 36 tickets, confirm the 7 clusters, and extract the 6 high-priority customer questions. Create a shared document with the exact customer wording from each ticket.

**Week 2: Draft answer shells.** For each of the 6 high-priority questions, create a draft FAQ entry with the customer question as the title and the placeholder "Draft answer - support team should add the verified resolution before publishing" as the body. Do not add product details, UI paths, or capability claims yet.

**Week 3-4: Support team verification.** Assign each draft FAQ entry to a support team member who handles that topic area. They should:

- Confirm the question is accurate based on their experience with customer tickets
- Research the current product capability or workaround
- Write a clear, step-by-step answer using the product's current UI and features
- Test the answer by following their own steps
- Flag any product gaps or bugs

**Week 5: Review and publish.** A content or support lead reviews all verified answers for consistency, clarity, and accuracy. Publish the entries to your help center or FAQ page.

**Week 6 and beyond: Observe and refine.** Track the metrics listed above. If an FAQ entry is not being used or if customers continue to ask the same question, schedule a review meeting to revise the answer or investigate visibility.

This workflow ensures that FAQ entries are grounded in real customer questions from the uploaded tickets and that answers are verified before publication.

## Why support ticket analysis matters for FAQ planning

Support tickets are a direct signal of customer confusion. When 4 customers ask the same question, that is not a coincidence—it is a gap in your help center, product UI, or onboarding. The 36 uploaded tickets show 7 distinct gaps, with 6 of them appearing frequently enough to warrant FAQ entries.

By analyzing support tickets, your team can:

- **Identify high-volume questions** — The 6 clusters with 4 tickets each represent the most common gaps.
- **Use real customer language** — FAQ entries that use the exact wording from tickets will match how customers search and ask questions.
- **Prioritize limited resources** — Focus on the 24 tickets in high-volume clusters before addressing the 12 one-off questions.
- **Create a verification process** — Draft answer shells ensure that support team members review and verify each answer before it goes live.
- **Observe impact** — Track whether FAQ entries are being used and where answers need refinement.

The uploaded tickets are a snapshot of customer questions at a point in time. As your product and customer base evolve, new questions will appear in your support queue. Treat this ticket analysis as the first pass, and plan to repeat the process as new patterns emerge.

## Next steps for your team

The 36 uploaded tickets reveal a clear roadmap for FAQ work. Start with the six high-volume clusters (reporting export, dashboard freshness, SSO setup, permissions and seats, API and webhooks, integration sync). Use the exact customer wording from the tickets as your FAQ question titles. Create draft answer shells and have your support team verify each one before publishing.

Once published, observe ticket volume, FAQ traffic, support response time, and customer feedback to understand how FAQ entries are being used. Use that data to prioritize the next round of FAQ work and to identify which answers need refinement.

This process turns your support queue into a knowledge base. Over time, your team will have a reference point for common issues and a clear process for turning new support tickets into FAQ entries.

allowed_claims: observed support-ticket clusters and counts from the uploaded rows
customer wording copied from the uploaded tickets
metrics to watch after publishing, without claiming those metrics improved

CSV export missing fields Why is the campaign CSV export missing source and owner columns?

Dashboard export permissions Why can analysts view dashboards but not export reports?
Dashboard stale data Why has our pipeline dashboard not refreshed since yesterday?
Warehouse sync lag How do we check whether the warehouse sync is delayed?

Why is the campaign CSV export missing source and owner columns?

forbidden_claims: prevented tickets or fewer repeat questions
churn, retention, upgrade, referral, ROI, or capacity outcomes
claims that FAQ entries help customers find answers or avoid tickets
claims that customers can find answers themselves because an FAQ exists
faster resolution or customers finding answers without opening tickets
claims that FAQ entries reduce how often the team answers a question
claims that FAQ entries perform better in search without measured evidence
claims that lower repeat tickets prove an FAQ entry is working
concrete answer steps, UI paths, menu names, or capability claims without resolution evidence

rows.description: Analysis of 36 uploaded support tickets reveals 7 customer question clusters. Learn which FAQ gaps your team should prioritize and how to turn tickets into review-ready answers.

faq.answer: The 36 uploaded tickets cluster into 7 distinct question areas: reporting export (4 tickets), dashboard freshness (4), SSO setup (4), permissions and seats (4), API and webhooks (4), integration sync (4), and miscellaneous (12). Reporting export and dashboard freshness are the highest-volume gaps that should become FAQ entries first.
faq.question: What are the most common customer questions in our support tickets?

faq.answer: Extract the customer's exact wording from the ticket, identify the core question, and create a draft answer shell. The support team then verifies the resolution before publishing. This process preserves customer language and ensures accuracy without guessing at product capabilities.
faq.question: How do I turn support tickets into FAQ entries?

faq.answer: Prioritize by observed ticket volume. Reporting export, dashboard freshness, SSO setup, permissions and seats, API and webhooks, and integration sync each appear 4 times in the 36 uploaded tickets. These six clusters represent 24 of the 36 tickets and should be addressed before lower-volume one-off questions.
faq.question: Which support ticket clusters should we prioritize for FAQ creation?

faq.answer: Track repeat ticket volume by cluster, FAQ page traffic, support team response time, and new customer self-service rates. Monitor these metrics after publication to see whether FAQ entries are being used and where answers may need refinement.
faq.question: What should we measure after publishing FAQ entries?
